exemplar.title: Anchoring effect in the real world (a partial replication of Kahneman and Tverskyâs (1974) study)

detailedExemplarData.title: Anchoring effect in the real world (a partial replication of Kahneman and Tverskyâs (1974) study)

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of aim linking anchoring bias to System 1 thinking
Accurate description of dual-process theory and its relevance to the study
weaknesses: Dependent variable not fully operationalized with units or scoring rules
Abrupt shifts in the narrative disrupt cohesion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates a solid grasp of theory and relevance, with an explicit aim and model connection meriting the upper half of the 3â4 band. However, the incomplete operationalization of the DV and occasional lack of flow prevent top-band performance, supporting a 4/6.

strengths: Research design is named (âloose replicationâ) with some rationale provided
Convenience sampling and participant characteristics are described
weaknesses: Lacks clear randomization procedure for group assignment
Materials list omits sample questionnaire items
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the design, sampling, and control variables are described, key procedural details (randomization, specific materials) are missing. This places the work in the lower half of the 1â2 band for exploration, yielding 2/4.

strengths: Appropriate choice of Mann-Whitney U test and descriptive statistics
Interpretation of findings is coherent and linked back to the hypothesis
weaknesses: Graphical presentation has missing axis labels and inconsistent units
Terminology and justification for some inferential tests contain conceptual errors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical methods are appropriate but contain procedural slips, and the graphing has notable faults. However, the clear interpretation of results places this at the lower end of the 3â4 band, for 3/6.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are identified and their relevance to results is explained
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified
weaknesses: Discussion of findings remains largely descriptive with limited critical evaluation
External validity considerations (e.g., sample generalizability) are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows good identification and explanation of strengths, limitations, and modifications, but the findings discussion lacks depth and critical analysis. This aligns with the midâband 3â4 descriptor, for a score of 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph of means and three box-and-whisker plots directly address the hypothesis (high vs low anchor).  However, axis titles, units and legends are partially missing or inconsistent, and one outlier is removed without graphical annotation.  These errors place the work in the 3â4 band, score 3.

The student summarises descriptive trends, cites U values and p-values, and explicitly states whether to accept/reject each hypothesis, linking this back to anchoring bias.  Interpretation is coherent and consistently relates statistical output to the research question; minor wording imprecision is present but does not undermine accuracy, so a strong performance within the 5â6 band (score 5) is justified.

Both IV (anchor level: student number 3 vs 91) and DV (participantsâ numerical estimates) are identified correctly, but the DV is not fully operationalised for each question (no units or scoring rule are explicitly stated).  Therefore the work meets the 3â4 descriptor â variables correctly named but not fully operationalised â and earns the lower end of that band.

Convenience sampling is identified and reasons for choosing Year-10 non-psychology students are given (knowledge level, availability, reduction of demand characteristics).  This constitutes an explanation of both technique and choice, aligning with the 3â4 band; detail is acceptable but not exhaustive, warranting 3/4.

Anchoring bias and the dual-process (System 1 vs System 2) framework are accurately described, and the narrative makes it clear how these concepts underpin the present replication.  The description contains no serious factual errors, and the connection to the planned manipulation (high vs low anchor) is explicitly articulated, albeit briefly; this satisfies the descriptor for the top band, though with room for richer depth, so 5/6 is awarded.

The discussion references Kahneman & Tversky and dual-process theory while attempting to explain why certain questions showed stronger effects.  The treatment is mainly descriptive with limited depth or critical engagement, meeting the 3â4 descriptor but not reaching the comprehensive âdiscussedâ standard of 5â6; 3/4 awarded.

The student states a clear aim â to examine the influence of an anchor on System 1 thinking â and goes on to outline why this matters (decision making mistakes, potential applications in education).  The link between the bias and real-world relevance is explicit and goes beyond a mere mention, though the explanation is somewhat general rather than deeply analytical; hence a solid but not perfect performance within the 5â6 band is appropriate.

Several strengths (random questions, controlled environment) and limitations (small convenience sample, excessive time) are identified and clearly explained in relation to the investigation and potential influence on results, fulfilling the requirement for explanation.  While not exhaustive, the coverage is coherent and relevant, meriting placement in the top band with a mid-level score of 5.

A table lists several extraneous and confounding variables with controls, and materials are itemised.  The controls are briefly justified (e.g., removing posters); this moves beyond description to explanation, matching the 3â4 band level, meriting 3/4 due to concise rather than comprehensive commentary.

Proposed changes (shorter response window, unfamiliar lab setting, larger/diverse sample) are explicitly tied to stated limitations (over-thinking, distractions, lack of generalisability) and their expected impact is justified, aligning with the descriptor âexplicitly linked and fully justifiedâ.  Quality is good though not exceptional, so 5/6 is appropriate.

The student labels the study a âloose replicationâ, indicates it is an independent-measures questionnaire with time limits, and explains seating and deception choices.  Rationale (reducing âscrew-you effectâ, focusing attention) is briefly provided, fulfilling the requirement for an explanation rather than mere description, hence band 3â4; depth is moderate, so 3/4.

Appropriate descriptive statistics and a Mann-Whitney U test for two independent groups are used, with an attempted Kruskal-Wallis follow-up.  Calculations are shown but there are conceptual slips (calling groups âvariablesâ, unclear justification for KW test).  Thus statistics are appropriate but contain errors, placing the work in the 3â4 band at the lower end.

annotations.comment: Units for Question 2 (e.g., â15.68Mâ) are inconsistent and ambiguous. Clarify whether âMâ stands for million and apply consistently.
The narrative explains patterns in the descriptive data but conflates groups with variables. Use precise terminology when discussing statistical results.
Outlier identification is rigorous and justified, demonstrating sound understanding of IQR methods.
The bar graph lacks clear axis titles, units, and legend explanation. Ensure all graphical elements are fully labeled.
Terminology in the inferential methods description is imprecise (âtwo variablesâ); specify independent and dependent samples clearly.
The student accurately distinguishes between System 1 and System 2 thinking and situates anchoring bias within this dual-process framework, showing strong conceptual understanding.
Definitions are well cited but the flow is disrupted by abrupt topic shifts. Consider adding transitional sentences to improve cohesion and readability.
The final sentence of the introduction is incomplete, cutting off the studentâs rationale. Ensure full sentences that clearly articulate the investigationâs relevance.
The aim is clearly stated and directly ties anchoring to System 1 engagement, presenting a focused research question.
The alternate hypothesis correctly identifies the IV and DV, but the dependent variable is not operationalized with units or scoring rules. Specify how estimates will be measured.
To operationalize your DV, state the exact unit (e.g., numeric percentage or millions) and describe how responses will be scored.
Design is labeled a loose replication with rationale (screw-you effect, focus) explicitly provided, showing good methodological awareness.
The description lacks detail on how participants were randomly assigned to anchor groups. Include a clear randomization procedure.
Convenience sampling is justified by availability and knowledge level, but potential sampling biases (e.g., homogeneity) are not discussed.
The table of extraneous and confounding variables clearly identifies controls, reflecting careful consideration of internal validity.
Confounding variables are labeled âNAâ for control; you should propose methods (e.g., screening for prior knowledge) to mitigate them.
Materials list includes ethical forms and questionnaire but omit specific question examples. Consider appending sample items to clarify materials.
Use of mean, median, and IQR demonstrates appropriate descriptive techniques that address central tendency and dispersion.
Report exact p-values and U statistics for all tests in the main text, rather than summarizing only significance, to enhance transparency.
The discussion appropriately references Kahneman & Tversky and attempts to link results to dual-process theory.
Theoretical links are descriptive rather than critically evaluated; consider discussing alternative explanations or boundary conditions.
The suggestion to reduce response time is directly linked to the limitation of overthinking and is well justified.
The sample limitation is identified but lacks discussion of how it affects external validity and generalizability.
The conclusion succinctly summarizes partial support for the hypothesis and acknowledges limitations, reflecting a balanced closure.
To deepen your conclusion, elaborate on specific practical implications and propose detailed directions for future research.
